rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4,6-trimethoxyacetophenone). This example clearly illustrates the key transformation: the formation of a C(aryl)-C(=O)R bond from an arene and a nitrile, which is the defining feature of this named reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple, acyclic nitrile (acetonitrile) and a highly activated, non-fused aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) to avoid overlap with more general Friedel-Crafts acylation reactions, which typically use acyl halides or anhydrides. The use of a nitrile as the electrophile precursor is specific to the Houben-Hoesch reaction. The simple substrates avoid complex motifs that could trigger other named reaction queries.
